clean_text: There is no safar. There are no owls. There is no contagion. This means that these things are not to be taken in the way that they were commonly understood and believed in the time of Jah in the time prior to the prophet sallall.aii alaihi wasallam's prior to his messengership Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala sent our prophet sallall.aii alaihi wasallam in a very dark time in human history. A time when the majority of humanity had fallen into the clutches of a shaitan and he came to release them from those clutches and to release them from the from the path of damnation that the majority of humanity was upon. And so there were certain beliefs at that time. There were certain things that were commonly known as kind of truisms or cultural idioms etc. And the prophet sallallah.aii Wasam and this narration and in others it is invalidating that perception not invalidating necessarily the actual physical thing that that perception or that superstition is attached to. So he says there is no ad meaning contagion and it doesn't mean that the prophet sallall.aii wasallam doesn't affirm that infectious diseases can't be transmitted and cannot be contagious. This is something that every person who observes an illness and observes it in any group of people will come to the conclusion that yes this is something that is contagious. If a person is ill with this particular illness and they interact physically with someone else who is healthy that they may that they may infect them that this illness may be contagious. However, the beliefs of the Mushri, the beliefs of the Jahidi Arabs at the time were a step beyond this amount or this portion of reason. It was a step beyond that. They believed in contagion so strongly, so earnestly that they did not believe that it was something to the will of Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala that it was something that once it had begun to spread that it will cause infection no matter what and that it is not something that is by the decree of Allah that it is by the will and permission of Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala and this is where the difference between the Muslim and the non really shows itself with respect to something a contagious illness. A believer understands that even if he is exposed to an illness, he can still not be harmed except by the will of Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala. And that if a person has been willed by Allah to become ill that he doesn't need a contagion for that to happen that it will happen by the will of Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala no matter how far away he may be from that particular illness. A man asked the prophet sallall.aii wasallam about this very thing. He said, and this is an extension of a narration similar to this where the prophet sallall.aii wasallam said that a contagion in that sense and in that way is a myth. Superstition largely superstitious. And so one of the companions said, "Oh messenger of Allah, we observe that if a man has a sick camel, a camel, that is a is a type of illness. it's kind of ringorm for for camels. then that if it is put into a pen with other healthy camels that they get sick. We observe this. We see this. The prophet sallall.aii Wasam said, "If that is the case and it is as believed at that time, then if it is not by the will of Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala, then how did the first camel get sick?" What we would refer to as patient zero. How did that happen? And so illness even though it has biological and physical contraptions that we can look at as cause and effect and can become contagious etc. This does not exempt it from the will of Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala. It is still by the will of Allah. And a person will only be afflicted with that which Allah has written upon him. And a person who has not been willed by Allah to become ill will not become ill. Now this doesn't mean that a person is you know irresponsible. No, the companions observed great caution with respect to very contagious diseases because they knew that the prophet sallallah.aii alaihi wasallam told them that if you hear of a plague or a contagion in a land do not go to it and do not leave it if you are there at that land so as not to spread it. However, still a person believes in the decree of Allah. This does not inter interfere with the decree of Allah because also one of the beliefs even amongst some of the Muslim is that a person may be killed or may become ill or die in adolescence and that all children or all youth who die in adolescence or die in their youth that this somehow now was before Allah had willed for them to die. And so they still have time that they that Allah had written for them. And so they will do things like haunt the places where they were. Right? If a if a child dies in a in a particular home that now that child's soul will continue to haunt the hallways of that home right until its time is up that Allah had originally written of old age whatever 50 60 years and then it will die then the soul is taken as if we were created and our souls were created to hang out in you know people's basement and haunt them haunt the new homeowners until our time is up. Or if a person is murdered before their time or if a person is killed in some kind of horrific accident that this person's spirit will continue to haunt. And these are things that even I remember growing up that the elders back in back in in Palestine and Jordan, these places used to say and used to teach us. And these are nothing more than superstitions. Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala takes this all back to the general statement of he says and there are no valid omens. There are no valid true omens. First of all, the idea of an omen is an indicator of something that is to occur in the future. Right? that if I'm leaving to go on a journey and a bird flies off and it takes a right, it banks right, okay, continue with my journey is going to be a good journey. And if it banks left, I better turn around and plan this for a different day because something very terrible is going to happen. Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala says, Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala has said none knows what is in the future except for Allah. None in the heavens or the earth. And Allah has repeated throughout the Quran and indicated in no uncertain terms and said explicitly that only Allah knows what the future holds. Only Allah knows what the future holds and those whom Allah informs, those whom Allah makes privy to that knowledge. Therefore, these so-called omens of the behaviors of certain animals and that these animals are going to reflect something ominous to come or something good to come. This is pure fable. This is pure superstition. This is pure nonsense that a shaitan whispers into the hearts of men and into the minds of men in order to weaken their resolve and obstruct their relationship and path to Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala. What do we understand from this? We understand that fasting and fasting this act of worship is so beloved to Allah is so integral to our journey to Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala that he has made it something that is a part of every single nations s worship of Allah that he has made it an obligatory factor, an obligatory element in their journey to Allah that every nation that has preceded us, every pro, every messenger who has come to his people, he has taught them some iteration of the five pillars of Islam and fasting is no different. It is a part and an integral part of every Muslim's journey to Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala. It may not have been in the exact same way with the exact same rules and parameters that fasting is as we know it today as the of Muhammad Sam. Wasam, but no doubt similar. And the concept of fasting, refraining from certain things, food and drink, namely sexual pleasure during the daylight hours, to sacrifice this for the pleasure of Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala. This is something that Allah loves dearly from his servant and he subhanahu wa ta'ala has obligated it upon every nation that has preceded us and he subhanahua has made it a prerequisite to tawa said in order that you may obtain tawa in order that you may shield yourselves successfully from the punishment of Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala. Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala then and of course fasting can mean a lot of things and fasting at its face seems very impending something that is very difficult for people to do and so this verse may elicit some feelings of apprehension Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala says in the following verse to quell any concerns that this fasting may be something that is too difficult for us to do that it is something too imposing. He says a number of days that are numbered. It's not most of the year, not most of the time, not even the majority of the time or even half or a substantial portion, merely a few days in the grand scheme of things. Contrasted with the rest of the year, it is a very small portion, but it is a very substantial portion. Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala has obligated fasting upon us and he has identified the month. He says, "Subhan Ramadan, this is the month of Ramadan in which the Quran was first revealed, in which the Quran was first sent down upon our messenger Muhammad sallall.aii wasallam." And so this month of Ramadan was something that is a known month from the lunar calendar that the Arabs of Jahedia considered the ninth month of the lunar calendar in that time and was hence affirmed by Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala and his messenger as the ninth month of the Islamic lunar calendar and it was referred to as the month of Ramadan from Ramblah which is the desert sand when it bakes and becomes very hot. It's referred to as Ramadan in order to that the Arabs viewed it as a time of year that is usually a very hot very difficult time of year with respect to heat. And the thing that is most prominent, the most unanimous and ubiquitous of feelings and emotions during the month of Ramadan is one of thirst, one of heat. And so Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala named this month in the Quran. It's the only month that is named that is mentioned by name in the Quran which is interesting because not even the four sacred months are mentioned by name. They are alluded to. They are referred to as the sacred months. However, Ramadan is not one of those four sacred months. And so Allah mentioned it by name in the Quran in order to raise its level and make Ramadan as a prelude in order for us to prepare ourselves for the coming sacred months. And so the month of Ramadan is a month that was known to the Jahia Arabs in pre-Islamic times. It's not something new to them. And also the concept of fasting was not new to them either. And so Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala chose this month 112th of a person's entire year It is not too little to where it has no effect on a person's year. Nor is it too much to where it impedes a person's livelihood. Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala has made it consequential but not imposing. Allah tells us that he desires ease for us and does not desire difficulty for us. However, more important than our ease is our relationship with Allah and that it is built upon a level of sacrifice and that is what Ramadan really is about. However, Ramadan as we have heard is a beloved month to Allah. It is a month of great baraka, great blessings from Allah. It is a month that is given sacredness in its own right. It is a month that is to act as a prelude to the three consecutive sacred months that follow. And the month of Ramadan is a month in which we practice this act of worship, this fasting. Very important. Why is it so critical? Because the act of fasting is a worship that brings a person very near to Allah and acts as a stepping stone for a person to be elevated in his or her righteousness. Our prophet sallall.aii Wasam has said in the narration of Abi collected by both Bkari and Muslim. He said, he said that Allah himself has said all of the deeds of the human being are potentially for him except for fasting. It is only for me and I personally reward the servant for a successful fast. What does that mean? All of the deeds are for him except for fasting. Now the scholars of Islam have mentioned a couple of interpretations to this narration. All of them are correct. And one of those is that in any act of worship that a person does, the remaining four or remaining pillars of Islam, a person could potentially use these acts of worship to gain some kind of worldly notoriety. person may pray and show off in his prayer. A person may give generously in zak and in order to be praised as generous. A person may travel for Hajj and Omrah in order to be praised as a Hajj and someone who has made this very difficult and sacrificial pilgrimage except for fasting. Fasting is a secret between you and Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala. No one knows if you are truly fasting except for you and Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala. Imagine person could eat a five course meal and then come out and be just as hungry as everyone else and be just as fasting as everyone else is. However, if a person observes his fast and refrains from those things which break a person's fast in private as he does in public, this is an indication that he truly believes in Allah. The only reason is because he understands that this is for Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala. And so if a person truly is fasting privately as he is publicly, this is something which is very private between himself and Allah. And so Allah will maintain that privacy on the day of judgment and personally reward a person for his fasting. Except for fasting, it is for me and I personally will reward the servant for fasting. He will not commission an angel to reward the person for fasting. He himself will reward a person for fasting in order to maintain this secrecy and this privacy and this intimacy that the person had established in his life in the dunya. Who knows if you have broken your woo or not? There's no apparent physical difference between a person who is in a state of wuboo and a person who is not. This act of worship though it goes a lot further than just refraining from eating and drinking. I mean, when you think about it, not eating anything, not drinking anything during the daylight hours, it's something that maybe could be difficult. It's something that could be challenging, but not really. It's not really that challenging. As a matter of fact, most people can do it. Soccer moms do it so that they can trim down for the summer. It's not something particularly impressive that a person refrain from eating and drinking. What's impressive is that a person refrain from the prohibitions of Allah. That's really where the rubber meets the road. That's really the essence of fasting. Our prophet Sam has said he said true fasting is not that a person refrain from eating and drinking. True fasting is that a person refrain from that which is prohibited and refrain from that which wastes a person's time, that which is inconsequential, that which is unnecessary. That is true fasting. Imagine that. Imagine with me that during every moment of your daytime hours during the month of Ramadan, you didn't change anything about your life. The only thing you did was refrain during those hours from anything haram and anything that will not gain benefit in this life or the hereafter. And you do that for 30 days consecutively. Everything's going to change suddenly for me in Ramadan. I'm going to immediately as soon as the sun sets on the 29th or the 30th day of Shabban immediately I'm going to wake up out of bed the next day a completely changed person. This is not how change happens. No change worth having happens this way. Change is something that happens incrementally. Change is something that happens with discipline and over the passage of time. Doesn't happen overnight. Person doesn't suddenly wake up a different person. This is something that has to be practiced and worked towards. And a person has to trip and fall and stumble on the path of change before they are able to truly embrace it. summary: This lecture discusses Islamic teachings on superstitions and false beliefs, specifically addressing the hadith 'There is no safar, there are no owls, there is no contagion.' The speaker explains that the Prophet Muhammad rejected pre-Islamic Arab superstitions about contagion, omens, and supernatural occurrences, clarifying that while infectious diseases can be transmitted through natural means, ultimate control over illness remains with Allah's will. The lecture emphasizes that believers must take reasonable precautions while maintaining faith in divine decree. The second portion transitions to discuss the importance and rulings of fasting in Ramadan, explaining it as a beloved pillar of Islam prescribed to all previous nations, designed to cultivate taqwa (God-consciousness), and highlighting its unique spiritual significance as a secret act between the servant and Allah.
suggested_title: Refuting Superstitions and the Spiritual Significance of Fasting in Ramadan
